article_title: Penn State Team Reveals Iron Telluride Is a Superconductor After Decades of Misidentification as an Ordinary Magnetic Metal

content_preview.title: Penn State Team Reveals Iron Telluride Is a Superconductor After Decades of Misidentification as an Ordinary Magnetic Metal
content_preview.summary: Two back-to-back Nature papers show that removing excess iron atoms from iron telluride unlocks superconductivity at 13.5 Kelvin, overturning a longstanding assumption and suggesting similar hidden quantum states may exist in other correlated materials.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Iron telluride (FeTe), a compound of iron and tellurium that researchers have studied for decades as an antiferromagnetic metal, is in fact a superconductor. A team led by Penn State physicist Cui-Zu Chang demonstrated that removing excess iron atoms from the material's crystal lattice suppresses its magnetism and switches on zero-resistance electrical conduction at approximately 13.5 Kelvin. The findings, reported in two papers [published simultaneously in Nature](https://www.psu.e...

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent condensed matter physics article. Clear narrative from misidentification to discovery, with moire engineering companion paper well integrated.
editor_notes.source_verification: 3 sources checked. Penn State: VERIFIED — confirms Cui-Zu Chang, two Nature papers April 1 2026, MBE on SrTiO3, Te vapor annealing, 13.5K Tc, moire superlattices, Cooper-pair droplet patterns. Phys.org: VERIFIED — confirms Chang quote verbatim ('excess iron atoms had disguised its superconductivity'), decades-old misclassification. PhysicsWorld: VERIFIED — confirms Te vapor annealing process, spin-orbit coupling, topological superconductivity candidate, Majorana zero modes, Pengcheng Dai Rice commentary.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified: 13.5K critical temperature, two simultaneous Nature papers, MBE methodology, stoichiometric ratio, moire engineering, topological implications. Chang and Dai quotes confirmed.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and scientifically precise. No sensationalism despite significant finding.
editor_notes.originality: No prior iron telluride or FeTe superconductivity coverage. Distinct from UH superconductivity record article.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Outstanding materials science article. All sources verified. Approved.
